text: India won the ICC Cricket World Cup in 2011, co-hosted with Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, defeating Sri Lanka in the final at Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai.
Australia has won the ICC Men's Cricket World Cup a record five times: 1987, 1999, 2003, 2007, and 2015.
Sachin Tendulkar is the all-time leading run-scorer in both Test and One Day International (ODI) cricket, and the only player to score 100 international centuries.
Brian Lara holds the record for the highest individual score in Test cricket, scoring 400 not out for the West Indies against England in 2004.

In cricket, a batsman is dismissed 'leg before wicket' (LBW) if the ball would have hit the stumps but is instead blocked by the batsman's leg, subject to specific conditions about pitching and impact.
The 1983 Cricket World Cup final saw India, captained by Kapil Dev, defeat the two-time defending champions West Indies at Lord's, a result considered one of the greatest upsets in cricket history.
The Indian Premier League (IPL), founded in 2008, is a Twenty20 franchise cricket league and one of the most-watched and highest-paying cricket leagues in the world.
Muttiah Muralitharan of Sri Lanka is the leading wicket-taker in both Test and ODI cricket history.
England won their first ICC Men's Cricket World Cup title in 2019, defeating New Zealand in a final decided by a Super Over after both the match and the Super Over ended in a tie.
A standard Test match is played over a maximum of five days, with each team batting twice (two innings), while ODIs are limited to 50 overs per side and T20Is to 20 overs per side.
The highest team total in ODI cricket history is 498/4, scored by England against the Netherlands in 2022.
The Ashes is a Test cricket series played between England and Australia, contested since 1882, named after a mock obituary for English cricket published after Australia's first win on English soil.
Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup a record five times: 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002.
Argentina won the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar, defeating France on penalties after a 3-3 draw, with Lionel Messi named the tournament's best player.
Lionel Messi and Cristiano Ronaldo are widely regarded as two of the greatest footballers of their generation, combining for a record number of Ballon d'Or awards between them.
The fastest goal in World Cup history was scored by Hakan Şükür of Turkey, just 11 seconds into a match against South Korea in the 2002 World Cup.
In football (soccer), a standard match consists of two 45-minute halves, and a team can be reduced to fewer than 11 players if a player is sent off with a red card.
The 1950 World Cup final match (the 'Maracanazo') saw Uruguay defeat host nation Brazil 2-1 in front of a crowd of roughly 200,000 at the Maracanã Stadium.
The UEFA Champions League is Europe's premier club football competition; Real Madrid holds the record for the most titles, with 15 as of 2024.
Miroslav Klose of Germany is the all-time leading goalscorer in FIFA World Cup history, with 16 goals across the 2002, 2006, 2010, and 2014 tournaments.
The FIFA World Cup was first held in 1930 in Uruguay, which also won the inaugural tournament as hosts.
The offside rule states an attacking player is offside if they are nearer to the opponent's goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent when the ball is played to them.
Pelé, who played for Brazil and Santos, is the only player to win three FIFA World Cups, in 1958, 1962, and 1970.
The English Premier League, founded in 1992, is one of the most-watched football leagues in the world, broadcast to over 180 countries.
The Boston Celtics hold the record for the most NBA championships, with 18 titles as of 2024, surpassing the Los Angeles Lakers.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar held the NBA all-time scoring record for nearly 39 years until LeBron James surpassed it in February 2023.
Michael Jordan won six NBA championships with the Chicago Bulls in the 1990s and was named NBA Finals MVP in all six of those title runs.
Wilt Chamberlain holds the NBA record for most points scored in a single game, with 100 points for the Philadelphia Warriors against the New York Knicks on March 2, 1962.
A standard NBA game consists of four 12-minute quarters, while FIBA (international) games consist of four 10-minute quarters.
The NBA was founded in 1946 as the Basketball Association of America (BAA) and adopted the name National Basketball Association in 1949 after merging with the National Basketball League.
The 1992 'Dream Team' was the first US Olympic basketball team to feature active NBA players, winning gold at the Barcelona Olympics by an average margin of over 40 points.
Stephen Curry holds the NBA record for most three-pointers made in a career, having redefined the game's emphasis on three-point shooting.
In the NBA, the shot clock gives a team 24 seconds to attempt a shot that at least hits the rim, a rule introduced in 1954 to speed up gameplay.
Novak Djokovic holds the record for the most Grand Slam men's singles titles in the Open Era, having surpassed Rafael Nadal and Roger Federer's totals.
Serena Williams won 23 Grand Slam singles titles in the Open Era, the most by any player, male or female, since 1968.
Wimbledon, first held in 1877, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only major still played on grass courts.
A standard tennis match is won by winning the majority of sets; men's Grand Slam matches (except the Australian and US Open) can go to five sets, while women's matches are best-of-three.
The longest tennis match in history was played at Wimbledon in 2010 between John Isner and Nicolas Mahut, lasting over 11 hours across three days, with a final set score of 70-68.
The four Grand Slam tournaments are the Australian Open, the French Open (Roland Garros), Wimbledon, and the US Open.
Rafael Nadal is nicknamed the 'King of Clay' for his dominance at the French Open, which he won a record 14 times.
In 1973, Billie Jean King defeated Bobby Riggs in the 'Battle of the Sexes' exhibition match, a landmark moment for women's sports.
A badminton match is played to the best of three games, with each game won by the first player or pair to reach 21 points (win by two, capped at 30).
Lin Dan of China is one of the most decorated badminton players in history, winning Olympic gold in singles at both the 2008 Beijing and 2012 London Olympics.
Badminton became an official Olympic sport at the 1992 Barcelona Olympics.
P.V. Sindhu of India became the first Indian woman to win an Olympic silver medal in badminton at the 2016 Rio Olympics, and later won bronze at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
The BWF (Badminton World Federation) World Championships is the sport's premier individual event, held annually except in Olympic years.
The fastest recorded badminton smash exceeds 500 km/h, making it one of the fastest-moving projectiles in any racquet sport.
Field hockey has been an Olympic sport since the 1908 London Games for men, and since 1980 for women.
India's men's field hockey team won gold at six consecutive Olympic Games from 1928 to 1956.
Major Dhyan Chand, an Indian field hockey player active in the 1920s-30s, is often referred to as 'The Wizard' for his exceptional goal-scoring and stick control.
A standard international field hockey match consists of four 15-minute quarters, and goals can only be scored from within the 'shooting circle' in front of the goal.
The Hockey World Cup, first held in 1971, is field hockey's premier international team event, organized by the FIH (International Hockey Federation).
India won the men's Hockey World Cup in 1975, its only title in the tournament's history to date.
In kabaddi, a 'raider' must cross into the opponent's half, tag or touch defenders, and return to their own half while holding a single breath, chanting 'kabaddi' continuously.
Kabaddi originated in ancient India and is one of the oldest team sports, with roots described in texts thousands of years old.
The Pro Kabaddi League (PKL), launched in 2014, is a professional kabaddi league in India that significantly boosted the sport's popularity and commercial value.
India's men's kabaddi team has historically dominated the Kabaddi World Cup and Asian Games competitions since the sport's international formalization.
A standard international kabaddi match is played between two teams of seven players each, with two halves of 20 minutes.
Lewis Hamilton and Michael Schumacher share the record for the most Formula 1 World Drivers' Championships, with seven titles each.
The first Formula 1 World Championship season was held in 1950, with the inaugural race taking place at Silverstone, United Kingdom.
Max Verstappen won a record 19 races in a single Formula 1 season in 2023, driving for Red Bull Racing.
Ferrari is the oldest and most successful constructor in Formula 1 history, having competed in every season since the championship began in 1950.
A Formula 1 Grand Prix weekend typically includes practice sessions, qualifying (which sets the starting grid), and the race itself, with points awarded to the top ten finishers.
The 1976 Formula 1 season featured a famous rivalry between Niki Lauda and James Hunt, with Hunt winning the drivers' championship by a single point.
Ayrton Senna, a three-time Formula 1 World Champion, is widely regarded as one of the greatest drivers in the sport's history; he died in an accident at the 1994 San Marino Grand Prix.
The modern Olympic Games were revived by Pierre de Coubertin, with the first modern Games held in Athens, Greece, in 1896.
Michael Phelps is the most decorated Olympian in history, with 28 total medals, including 23 golds, across swimming events from 2004 to 2016.
The Olympic Games are held every four years, alternating between the Summer and Winter Games every two years within that cycle.
Usain Bolt of Jamaica won gold in the 100m and 200m sprints at three consecutive Olympics (2008, 2012, 2016), a feat known as the 'triple-triple' when including relay golds.
The 1980 'Miracle on Ice' saw the amateur United States men's ice hockey team defeat the heavily favored Soviet Union at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics.
Tokyo hosted the Summer Olympics in 2021 (postponed from 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic), making it the first Olympics held without spectators in most venues.
Nadia Comăneci of Romania was the first gymnast in Olympic history to score a perfect 10, achieved at the 1976 Montreal Olympics.
Neeraj Chopra won India's first-ever Olympic gold medal in athletics, winning the javelin throw event at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
